title: How would you implement custom dependency injection in Spring for a complex scenario that the framework doesn't directly support?

answers.junior: You can use advanced Spring features to inject dependencies in special cases, like BeanPostProcessor or custom annotations.
answers.middle: BeanPostProcessor lets you modify beans during creation. Custom annotations and ApplicationContextAware allow manual injection. Use for legacy systems or complex graphs.
answers.senior: Custom DI uses BeanPostProcessor, reflection, and programmatic bean registration. Ensure thread safety and avoid circular dependencies. Document custom logic and test thoroughly.
answers.tricky: Custom DI is for integrating legacy systems or runtime-generated beans. Use BeanPostProcessor, ApplicationContextAware, and GenericApplicationContext. Validate with integration tests and document for maintainability.
